24: pisteuō; boethei mou te apistia — I believe; help my unbelief — one of the most theologically honest statements in the Gospels; the father's response is not a claim to complete faith but a simultaneous affirmation of what faith he has and an acknowledgment of its inadequacy. The prayer is self-aware about its own condition: the faith that asks for help with unbelief is already a form of faith (it turns to Christ rather than away from him). The raw honesty is characteristic of Mark's straightforward Christological realism.
1: amēn legō hymin hoti eisin tines tōn hōde hestēkotōn oitines ou mē geusōntai thanatou heōs an idōsin tēn basileian tou theou elēlythuian en dynamei — truly I tell you that there are some of those standing here who will not taste death until they see the kingdom of God having come in power — the saying has generated significant interpretive debate. Three main referents for the kingdom coming in power: (1) the Transfiguration immediately following (Mark 9:2–8); (2) the Resurrection and Pentecost; (3) the destruction of Jerusalem (AD 70). The Markan placement immediately before the Transfiguration supports reading it as a partial fulfillment — a preview of the Son of Man coming in the glory of the Father (Mark 8:38). en dynamei (in power) is the qualifier: not the kingdom's veiled presence in Jesus's current ministry but its manifest, undeniable expression.
2: kai metamorphōthē emprosthen autōn — and he was transfigured before them — metamorphoō (to transform in form — the source of the English "metamorphosis") in the passive (metamorphōthē, divine passive — he was transfigured by divine action) marks this as a revelation given, not a self-display. The form (morphē) becoming visible is the glory Jesus possesses as Son of God, momentarily uncloaked before three witnesses. The three (Peter, James, John) are the inner circle who will also witness the Gethsemane agony (Mark 14:33): the same three see the glory and the grief. meta hēmeras hex (after six days) is the precise temporal note; Luke 9:28 says "about eight days" — the difference may reflect inclusive vs. exclusive counting.
3: kai ta himatia autou egeneto stilbonta leuka lian, hoia gnapheys epi tēs gēs ou dynatai houtōs leukanai — and his garments became intensely gleaming white, such as a bleacher on earth cannot so whiten — stilbō (to flash, gleam, glitter — of radiant light) and leuka (white) together describe a luminosity beyond earthly process. The comparison to a fuller/bleacher (gnapheys, one who washes and whitens cloth) that cannot achieve the same result establishes the otherworldly quality. Daniel 7:9 describes the Ancient of Days's garment as "white as snow"; Revelation 1:14 describes Christ's hair similarly. The garments' transformation is the visible index of the divine glory being revealed.
4: kai ōphthē autois Ēlias syn Mōysei kai ēsan syllalountes tō Iēsou — and there appeared to them Elijah with Moses and they were talking with Jesus — ōphthē (appeared — aorist passive of horaō; the same verb used for resurrection appearances in 1 Corinthians 15:5–8) marks the visionary quality. Mark names Elijah before Moses — the eschatological order (Elijah as forerunner of the Day of the Lord; Malachi 4:5) rather than the chronological order. The two figures represent the prophets and the law — together encompassing the entirety of OT revelation, now converging on the One they both anticipated. Luke 9:31 specifies they spoke of Jesus's exodos — his departure/death in Jerusalem.
5: Rhabbi, kalon estin hēmas hōde einai, kai poiēsōmen treis skēnas, soi mian kai Mōysei mian kai Ēlia mian — Rabbi, it is good for us to be here; let us make three shelters — one for you, one for Moses and one for Elijah — Rhabbi (Teacher — Aramaic title for a recognized instructor) is Peter's address when he does not know how to respond. skēnē (tent, booth, tabernacle — the word of both the Feast of Tabernacles/Sukkot and the Wilderness Tabernacle housing the divine presence) may suggest a Sukkot-festival association, or simply that Peter wants to prolong the encounter. Three equal booths places Moses and Elijah on the same level as Jesus — a category-error that the voice from the cloud immediately corrects.
6: ou gar ēdei ti apokrithē, ekphoboi gar egenonto — for he did not know what to say, for they were terrified — Mark's frank admission of Peter's incomprehension: ēdei (he knew — imperfect, he was not knowing, could not find what to say). ekphobos (intensely afraid, out-of-one's-mind with fear — ek- + phobos = extreme fear) is the theophanic fear-response, not a mild unease. Peter's suggestion is offered out of incomprehension and dread. The verse is characteristic of Mark's honest portrayal of the disciples' limitations at moments of divine revelation.
7: kai egeneto nephelē episkiazousa autois, kai egeneto phōnē ek tēs nephelēs — and a cloud came overshadowing them, and a voice came from the cloud — nephelē (cloud) is the theophanic vehicle throughout the OT: the pillar of cloud in the wilderness (Exodus 13:21), the cloud of glory on Sinai (Exodus 19:9; Mark 24:15–18), the Shekinah-cloud filling the Temple (1 Kings 8:10–11). The divine presence arrives via the same vehicle as Sinai. Houtos estin ho huios mou ho agapētos, akouete autou — this is my beloved Son; listen to him — the declaration matches the baptism-voice (Mark 1:11) but adds akouete autou (listen to him): a citation of Deuteronomy 18:15 LXX (autou akousesthe = to him you shall listen), where Moses promised a coming prophet. The voice identifies Jesus as the eschatological Prophet of Deuteronomy 18 — the definitive speaker of divine words, superseding Moses himself.
8: kai exapina periblepsamennoi ouketi oudena eidon alla ton Iēsoun monon meth' heautōn — and suddenly looking around they no longer saw anyone except Jesus alone with them — exapina (suddenly, all at once) marks the abrupt end of the vision. Moses and Elijah have withdrawn; only Jesus remains. monon (alone, only) is the theological emphasis: Jesus alone remains as the one to hear and to follow. The Law (Moses) and the Prophets (Elijah) have been subsumed into the Son — the earlier modes of revelation have yielded to the final Word.
9: kai katabaiontes ek tou orous diesteilato autois hina mēdeni ha eidon diēgēsōntai, ei mē hotan ho huios tou anthrōpou ek nekrōn anastē — and as they came down from the mountain he charged them to tell no one what they had seen until the Son of Man rose from the dead — the Messianic Secret with a defined termination point: the silence is not permanent but until (heōs) the resurrection. ek nekrōn anastē (rose from the dead — standard resurrection formula) is stated plainly as the future event that will lift the prohibition. The Transfiguration cannot be correctly understood before the resurrection: a visionary preview of glory without the death that precedes it would be read as triumphalism rather than vindication-after-suffering.
10: kai ton logon ekratēsan pros heautous syzētountes ti estin to ek nekrōn anastēnai — and they kept the matter among themselves, debating what the rising from the dead meant — ekratēsan ton logon (held the matter/command to themselves — krateō = to grip, hold firmly) alongside syzētountes (debating together — iterative, ongoing discussion). The specific confusion is about ek nekrōn anastēnai (rising from among the dead). Jewish expectation had a general resurrection at the end of the age; the notion of a single individual rising before that general event was outside the standard framework. The disciples do not have a category for what Jesus has predicted.
11: kai epērōtōn auton legontes hoti legousin hoi grammateis hoti Ēlian dei elthein prōton — and they were asking him: why do the scribes say that Elijah must come first — the Malachi 4:5 expectation ("I will send you Elijah the prophet before the great and awesome day of the LORD") was standard scribal teaching. The disciples' question connects the Elijah-appearance on the mountain with the Elijah-must-come-first doctrine: if Elijah appeared but the Day of the LORD has not arrived, what does it mean? The question reflects genuine theological perplexity, not hostility.
12: Ho de ephē autois, Ēlias men elthōn prōton apokathistanei panta — and he said to them: Elijah indeed comes first and restores all things — Jesus affirms the Elijah-must-come-first doctrine. apokathistanei panta (restores all things — present tense, the settled expectation) uses the full restoration-word: apokathistēmi (to restore to the original state — apo- = back + kathistēmi = to establish; cf. Acts 3:21). kai pōs gegraptai epi ton huion tou anthrōpou hina polla pathē kai exoudēnōthē — and how is it written about the Son of Man that he must suffer many things and be treated with contempt — the counterquestion: the Son of Man's script in Scripture includes rejection and suffering. exoudēnoō (to treat as nothing — from ex- + ouden = to reduce to nothing, to despise utterly) is the word for total contempt, echoing the Suffering Servant language of Isaiah 53:3 LXX.
13: alla legō hymin hoti kai Ēlias elēlythen, kai epoiēsan autō hosa ēthelon, kathōs gegraptai ep' auton — but I tell you that Elijah has also come, and they did to him whatever they wanted, as it is written about him — the identification of John the Baptist as the fulfillment of the Elijah expectation (explicit in Matthew 11:14; Mark 17:13; implicit here). elēlythen (has come — perfect: the arrival is complete and the result stands). hosa ēthelon (whatever they wanted) parallels Ahab and Jezebel's persecution of Elijah (1 Kings 19:2,10) — John's imprisonment and beheading by Herod at Herodias's instigation maps onto the Elijah-Jezebel template. The Elijah-type has been fulfilled and rejected simultaneously.
14: Kai elthontes pros tous mathētas eidon ochlon polun peri autous kai grammateis syzētountas pros autous — and coming to the other disciples they saw a large crowd around them and scribes debating with them — the descent from the mountain's divine glory into the valley's controversy and failure is abrupt and structurally deliberate. On the mountain: the Father's declaration and divine presence; in the valley: scribal challenge and disciples' inability. The structural juxtaposition is Markan theology in narrative form: the glory of the Son is not yet accessible; the disciples operate in the struggle of the present age.
15: kai euthys pas ho ochlos idontes auton exethambethesan kai prostrechontes ēspazontos auton — and immediately all the crowd seeing him were overwhelmed with amazement and running to him they greeted him — ekthambeō (to be intensely amazed, overwhelmed — a strong compound emotion-word used at Mark 1:27; Mark 10:24,32; Mark 16:5,6) is the crowd's immediate response to Jesus's appearance. Some commentators propose that residual radiance from the Transfiguration explains the unusual amazement; Mark does not say so, leaving the reaction to the reader's inference.
16: kai epērōtēsen autous, Ti syzēteite pros autous? — and he asked them: what are you debating with them? — the open question invites the public account of the failed exorcism (Mark 9:17–18). The disciples' failure has become a public matter debated with the scribes rather than a private learning moment; the question exposes it before the crowd and sets up the teaching that follows.
17: kai apekrithē autō heis ek tou ochlou, Didaskale, ēnenka ton huion mou pros se, echonta pneuma alalon — and one from the crowd answered him: Teacher, I brought my son to you, having a mute spirit — pneuma alalon (a mute/speechless spirit — alalos = without speech, speechless) identifies the possessing spirit by its chief symptom. Verse 25 will add kōphon (deaf) to the description. The father frames the case as: my son has a spirit — the demonic agency is the cause; the boy is the victim. Didaskale (Teacher) is the crowd-address; the father does not yet call Jesus "Lord" (that comes in Mark 9:24's neighbor-verse, Mark 9:22).
18: kai hopou ean auton katalabē rēssei auton, kai aphrizei kai trizei tous odontas kai xērainetai — and wherever it seizes him it throws him down, and he foams and gnashes his teeth and becomes rigid — the triad of clinical symptoms: rēssō (to throw down, convulse violently), aphrizō (to foam at the mouth — aphros = foam), trizō tous odontas (to grind/gnash the teeth), xērainō (to become dry/rigid — the same verb as the withered hand of Mark 3:1). The symptom-portrait resembles what would be diagnosed as tonic-clonic seizure; the Markan narrative does not separate the demonological and medical dimensions but treats the spiritual reality as the underlying cause. kai eipa tois mathētais sou hina auto ekbalōsin, kai ouk ischysan — and I told your disciples to cast it out and they were not strong enough.
19: Ho de apokritheis autois legei, Ō genea apistos, heōs pote pros hymas esomai? heōs pote anexomai hymōn? — and answering he says to them: O unbelieving generation, how long shall I be with you? how long shall I put up with you? — genea apistos (faithless generation — the alpha-privative of pistos, faithful/trusting) is the diagnosis. The double heōs pote (how long?) echoes the wilderness-complaint texts where God laments the faithless people (Numbers 14:27 LXX: heōs pote tēn genean tautēn tēn ponēran). Jesus applies the Exodus-generation judgment to his contemporaries. The lament is genuine: the one who has repeatedly demonstrated divine power is surrounded by persistent unbelief.
20: kai ēnenkan auton pros auton, kai idōn auton to pneuma euthys synesparaxen auton, kai pesōn epi tēs gēs ekulieto aphrizōn — and they brought him to him, and seeing him the spirit immediately convulsed him, and falling on the ground he was rolling and foaming — euthys; the spirit's response to Jesus's presence is immediate and violent before any command is given. sysprassō (to convulse/tear together — the intensive compound) and kuliō (to roll, writhe) describe the extreme seizure at the moment of encounter. The violence at the sight of Jesus (not in response to a command) is the demonic counterpart of involuntary submission recorded in 1:24 and Mark 5:6–7: the unclean spirits recognize Jesus's authority and react with either obeisance or violence.
21: kai epērōtēsen ton patera autou, Possos chronos estin hōs touto gegonen autō? Ho de eipen, Ek paidiothen — and he asked his father: how long has this been happening to him? and he said: from childhood — possos chronos (how long a time — chronological duration) establishes the depth of the entrapment: not a recent onset but from infancy (paidiothen = from the time of being a small child). The pastoral attention to chronological detail is characteristic of Mark's healing accounts (Mark 5:25: twelve years; Mark 5:42: twelve years old). Jesus's interest in the history personalizes the encounter rather than treating the case as a generic exorcism.
22: kai pollakis kai eis pyr auton ebalen kai eis hydata hina apolesei auton — and many times it threw him into fire and water to destroy him — pollakis (many times, repeatedly) emphasizes the chronic destruction-attempts. The demonic intent is explicit: ina apolesei auton (in order to destroy him — apollymi = to destroy utterly). all' ei ti dynē, boēthēson hēmin splagchnistheis eph' hēmas — but if you can do anything, have compassion on us and help us — ei ti dynē (if you are able to do anything) is the conditional that challenges Jesus's power: the father has seen the disciples fail and qualified his faith accordingly. splagchnizomai is the gut-compassion appeal (cf. Mark 1:41; Mark 6:34; Mark 8:2). hēmin (on us) — the father identifies himself with his son: the suffering is shared.
23: Ho de Iēsous eipen autō, To ei dynē — panta dynata tō pisteuonti — and Jesus said to him: the "if you can" — all things are possible for the one who believes — Jesus echoes the father's conditional (ei ti dynē) back as a quoted phrase with an implicit incredulity: to ei dynē (the "if you can" — you are really framing this as a question about my capacity?). The capacity-question is redirected to a faith-question. panta dynata tō pisteuonti (all things are possible for the one who believes) is not a general faith-formula for any petition but a specific statement that the obstacle in this encounter is not Jesus's power but the presence or absence of faith that opens the work of God.
25: idōn de ho Iēsous hoti episyntrechei ochlos, epetimēsen tō pneumati tō akathartō legōn autō, To alalon kai kōphon pneuma, egō soi epitassō, exelthe ex autou kai mēketi eiselthēs eis auton — seeing that a crowd was running together, he rebuked the unclean spirit saying: you mute and deaf spirit, I command you, come out of him and never enter him again — Jesus addresses the spirit by its symptoms: alalon kai kōphon (mute and deaf). egō soi epitassō (I command you — the first-person emphatic egō + epitassō = to command with authority). The prohibition is permanent: mēketi eiselthēs eis auton (never enter him again) — this distinguishes the healing from cases where re-entry was possible; the cure is final.
26: kai kraxas kai polla sparaxas exēlthen, kai egeneto hōsei nekros, hōste tous pollous legein hoti apethanen — and crying out and greatly convulsing him it came out, and he became as if dead, so that most said he had died — the exit is violent: kraxas (the same exiting-shriek as Mark 1:26; Mark 5:7) and polla sparaxas (convulsing greatly). The post-exorcism collapse looks terminal to onlookers — hōsei nekros (as if dead). The condition creates a death-and-resurrection pattern: the boy who appeared dead will be raised by Jesus's hand in Mark 9:27, enacting the resurrection-pattern in miniature and anticipating the passion-resurrection predictions framing this scene.
27: ho de Iēsous kratēsas tēs cheiros autou ēgeiren auton, kai anestē — but Jesus taking him by the hand lifted him up, and he stood up — krateō tēs cheiros (taking by the hand) is the resurrection-gesture: the same action as raising Jairus's daughter (Mark 5:41: ekratēsen tēs cheiros tou paidiou). ēgeiren (lifted up, raised — the standard resurrection-verb egeirō) and anestē (stood up — the root of anastasis, resurrection). The sequence — collapsed as if dead → raised by the hand of Jesus → stood up — enacts the resurrection in miniature at the structural center of a chapter bounded by passion-predictions (Mark 8:31; Mark 9:31).
28: Kai eiselthontos autou eis oikon hoi mathētai autou kat' idian epērōtōn auton, Hoti hēmeis ouk ēdynasthēmen ekbalein auto? — and when he had entered a house his disciples privately asked him: why were we not able to cast it out? — the house/crowd spatial pattern again (cf. Mark 7:17; Mark 4:10). The disciples' question assumes they had genuine delegated authority to exorcise (consistent with Mark 6:7,13 where they were sent out and succeeded), and that this failure therefore requires explanation. The failure is not presented as routine.
29: kai eipen autois, Touto to genos en oudeni dynatai exelthein ei mē en proseuchē — and he said to them: this kind can come out by nothing except prayer — touto to genos (this kind/genus) implies a taxonomy of spiritual powers: some categories require specific conditions. proseuchē (prayer — directed communication toward God) alone is named; the UBS5/NA28 text does not include "and fasting" (some MSS add kai nēsteia as a secondary expansion). The implication: the disciples attempted the exorcism relying on delegated authority as a formula, without maintained communion with God through prayer. Authority over this category of demonic resistance requires ongoing dependence on God, not merely the use of Jesus's name.
30: Kakeithen exelthontes paraporeuonto dia tēs Galilaias, kai ouk ēthelen hina tis gnō — and leaving from there they were passing through Galilee, and he did not want anyone to know — the concealed Galilean passage is explicitly in service of teaching the disciples (edidasken gar tous mathētas autou = for he was teaching his disciples, Mark 9:31). The private teaching-journey mode is the deliberate counterpart to the public ministry of chapters 1–6. The content of the private teaching is the second passion prediction.
31: edidasken gar tous mathētas autou kai elegen autois hoti Ho huios tou anthrōpou paradidotai eis cheiras anthrōpōn, kai apoktanousin auton, kai apoktantheis meta treis hēmeras anastēsetai — for he was teaching his disciples and saying to them: the Son of Man is being delivered into the hands of men, and they will kill him, and having been killed after three days he will rise — paradidotai (is being delivered, betrayed — present tense, not future) frames the betrayal as a process already in motion. The passive marks Jesus as delivered rather than merely captured: someone is handing him over. eis cheiras anthrōpōn (into the hands of men) — the Son of Man delivered into human hands is the inversion of Daniel 7:13–14 where the Son of Man receives authority and all peoples are delivered into his hands. The three days are stated plainly: death is not the end of the prediction.
32: hoi de ēgnooun to rhēma kai ephobounto auton epērōtēsai — and they did not understand the saying and were afraid to ask him — ēgnooun (were ignorant of, did not comprehend — imperfect of agnoeō = not to know, the opposite of gnōsis) describes continuing incomprehension. The added detail: ephobounto auton epērōtēsai (they were afraid to ask him) — the fear prevents the clarifying question. They understand enough to be unsettled and not enough to process; fear seals the incomprehension rather than opening it to resolution. The disciples' trajectory from Caesarea Philippi through Gethsemane is one of increasing incomprehension and fear.
33: Kai ēlthon eis Kapharnaoun, kai en tē oikia genomenos epērōta autous, Ti en tē hodō dielogizesthe? — and they came to Capernaum, and being in the house he asked them: what were you debating on the road? — Capernaum is the Galilean base (Mark 1:21; Mark 2:1). The house-setting is the private teaching space. dielogizesthe (were you reasoning/debating together — the same root as dialogismos, Mark 2:6,8; Mark 8:16) is Jesus's probing question: the subject is not volunteered; he asks directly. The connection to Mark 9:34–35 is that the disciples' road-debate about greatness is placed in proximity to Jesus's passion-prediction — the juxtaposition is the verdict.
34: hoi de esiōpōn, pros allēlous gar dielechthēsan en tē hodō tis meizōn — and they were silent, for on the road they had argued with one another who was greatest — esiōpōn (they were silent — imperfect: sustained silence, the silence of shame). tis meizōn (who is greater) was the subject: a greatness-ranking argument. The juxtaposition is devastating: Jesus predicts his own death and delivery into human hands; the disciples debate their own relative precedence. The silence is not ignorance but shame — they recognize the incongruity they cannot verbalize.
35: kai kathisas ephōnēsen tous dōdeka kai legei autois, Ei tis thelei prōtos einai estai pantōn eschatos kai pantōn diakonos — and sitting down he called the Twelve and says to them: if anyone wants to be first he will be last of all and servant of all — kathisas (having sat — the posture of authoritative teaching; cf. Mark 13:3). diakonos (servant, table-server — later the term for the church office of deacon, derived from this and parallel teaching) is the inversion: the path to greatness runs through service, not rank. pantōn eschatos (last of all) and pantōn diakonos (servant of all) — the superlative-absolute forms: not "less great" but last and servant of everyone.
36: kai labōn paidion estēsen auto en mesō autōn kai enagkalisamenos auto eipen autois — and taking a little child he placed it in the middle of them and taking it in his arms he said to them — the embodied teaching: paidion (small child) is placed en mesō autōn (in the middle of them) as the visual object-lesson for the verbal teaching of Mark 9:35. enagkalizomai (to take in the arms, to embrace — used only here and 10:16 in the NT) is the action that accompanies the teaching: Jesus holds the child while speaking. The embrace embodies what he is teaching about reception of the insignificant.
37: hos an hen tōn toioutōn paidion dexētai epi tō onomati mou, eme dechetai, kai hos an eme dexētai, ouk eme dechetai alla ton aposteilanta me — whoever receives one of these little children in my name receives me; and whoever receives me does not receive me but the one who sent me — dechomai (to receive, welcome — the hospitality-reception verb). epi tō onomati mou (in my name — because they belong to me, on the basis of my identity). The chain: receiving the insignificant child = receiving Jesus = receiving the Father. The logic inverts the honor hierarchy: the socially invisible and powerless child is the site of encounter with God. The "sent" language (ton aposteilanta me) is the Johannine mission-chain (cf. John 13:20): to receive the sent-one is to receive the Sender.
38: Ephē autō ho Iōannēs, Didaskale, eidomen tina en tō onomati sou ekballonta daimonia, kai ekōlyomen auton, hoti ouk ēkolouthei hēmin — John said to him: Teacher, we saw someone casting out demons in your name, and we tried to stop him, because he was not following us — the motivation is explicit: hoti ouk ēkolouthei hēmin (because he was not following us — not "following you" but us). The disciples have made group-membership their criterion for valid ministry. The irony is structural: they have just failed to cast out a demon (Mark 9:18); this unaffiliated person succeeded in the same activity. Their failure generates a boundary-policing attempt; Jesus's response releases the boundary.
39: Ho de Iēsous eipen, Mē kōluete auton. Oudeis gar estin hos poiēsei dynamin epi tō onomati mou kai dynēsetai tachy kakologēsai me — and Jesus said: do not stop him. For no one who does a miracle in my name will be able soon to speak evil of me — mē kōluete (do not stop — the same verb the disciples used for their prohibition). The reason is psychological-theological: the use of Jesus's name in effective miracle-working creates an alignment with Jesus that makes immediate apostasy (kakologēsai me = to speak evil of me) logically incompatible with the act just performed. Effective ministry in Jesus's name creates a real if informal connection to him.
40: hos gar ouk estin kath' hēmōn, hyper hēmōn estin — for whoever is not against us is for us — the positive boundary: it is drawn at opposition, not formal discipleship. The saying stands in apparent tension with Matthew 12:30 / Luke 11:23 ("whoever is not with me is against me") but the contexts differ: Matthew 12 addresses explicit opposition to Jesus's exorcistic work; Mark 9 addresses independent ministry using Jesus's name. The criterion here is: absence of active opposition = at least implicitly aligned.
41: Hos gar an potisē hymas potērion hydatos en onomati hoti Christou este, amēn legō hymin hoti ou mē apolese ton misthon autou — for whoever gives you a cup of water in the name that you are Christ's, truly I tell you he will certainly not lose his reward — potērion hydatos (a cup of water — the minimum possible act of hospitality, a single cup). en onomati hoti Christou este (in the name/identity that you are of Christ — because the recipients bear the identity of belonging to the Messiah). The amēn oath-formula guarantees: no act of service toward those who belong to Christ goes unrewarded, however small. The saying follows directly from the child-in-the-middle teaching: even the cup of water given to the insignificant disciple counts.
42: Kai hos an skandalisē hena tōn mikrōn toutōn tōn pisteuontōn, kalon estin autō mallon ei perikeite mylos onikos peri ton trachēlon autou kai beblētai eis tēn thalassan — and whoever causes one of these little ones who believe to stumble, it is better for him if a large millstone were placed around his neck and he were thrown into the sea — skandalizō (to cause to stumble, to trip someone into sin or loss of faith — from skandalon = the trigger of a trap). hoi mikroi ... hoi pisteuontes (the little ones who believe — the vulnerable believers, those without social standing) are the protected group. mylos onikos (donkey-millstone — the large upper millstone of a donkey-driven mill, enormously heavy vs. the small hand-millstone) around the neck and drowning: the lesser fate compared to what awaits those who destroy the faith of the vulnerable.
43: Kai ean skandalizē se hē cheir sou, apokoson autēn — and if your hand causes you to stumble, cut it off — the escalating series (hand → foot → eye) uses the most radical physical imagery for the absolute seriousness of not allowing anything to impede entry into life. apokoson (cut off — decisive aorist imperative of apokoptō). The hyperbole is rhetorical rather than prescriptive — the OT never commanded literal self-mutilation — but the exaggeration makes the point: better to enter life without the offending limb than to enter Gehenna with all parts intact. hē zōē (life) vs. geenna (Gehenna) are the two destinies.
44: Verse 44 is absent in the best manuscripts (including Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, and Codex Washingtonianus) and is regarded by UBS5/NA28 as a scribal interpolation of the refrain from Mark 9:48. The phrase hopou ho skōlēx autōn ou teleutā kai to pyr ou sbennyetai (where their worm does not die and the fire is not quenched) is the Isaiah 66:24 citation; a copyist repeated it after Mark 9:43 to complete the rhetorical pattern, though the original text placed it only at Mark 9:48.
45: kai ean ho pous sou skandalizē se, apokoson auton — and if your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off — the foot continues the series: hand (the instrument of doing), foot (the instrument of going), eye (Mark 9:47, the instrument of seeing and desiring). The three members represent different modes of engagement in sin; each is named with the same absolute counsel. kalon estin eiselthein eis tēn zōēn chōlon ē tous duo podas echonta blēthēnai eis tēn geennan — better to enter life lame than having two feet to be thrown into Gehenna.
46: As with Mark 9:44, Mark 9:46 is absent in the best manuscript tradition and represents a scribal repetition of the Mark 9:48 refrain after Mark 9:45 to maintain the three-part rhetorical structure. UBS5/NA28 omits it. The Isaiah 66:24 citation belongs at Mark 9:48 where it closes the Gehenna-sayings as a whole.
47: kai ean ho ophthalmos sou skandalizē se, ekbale auton — and if your eye causes you to stumble, pluck it out — the third member: ekbale auton (pluck it out — the same verb as "cast out" for exorcisms; ekballō applied to one's own eye). The eye as the organ of desire and envy (ophthalmos ponēros = evil eye, Mark 7:22) is the final, most intimate member of the series. monophthalmon eiselthein eis tēn basileian tou theou ē duo ophtalmous echonta blēthēnai eis tēn geennan — the positive destination here is explicitly hē basileia tou theou (the kingdom of God), the term used throughout chapters 1–10 for the positive eschatological state.
48: hopou ho skōlēx autōn ou teleutā kai to pyr ou sbennyetai — where their worm does not die and the fire is not quenched — the citation is Isaiah 66:24, the closing verse of Isaiah: "they will go out and see the dead bodies of those who rebelled against me; for their worm will not die and their fire will not be quenched." geenna (Gehenna) is the Greek form of Hebrew Gē-Hinnom (Valley of Hinnom), the ravine south of Jerusalem associated with child-sacrifice to Molech (2 Kings 23:10; Jeremiah 7:31) and later used as the city's refuse dump with its perpetual fires. Jesus uses Gehenna as his consistent image for final judgment, and the Isaiah 66:24 citation — the last verse of the OT's longest prophetic book — provides the authoritative scriptural warrant. The worm and the fire are both inextinguishable: permanent, not temporary.
49: pās gar pyri halisthēsetai — for everyone will be salted with fire — among the most difficult sayings in Mark. The three main interpretations: (1) the purifying trial of persecution and suffering is the "salt" that preserves and refines every disciple — fire as purifying agent (cf. Malachi 3:2; 1 Peter 1:7); (2) the Gehenna-fire of Mark 9:43–48 will "salt" those who are judged — judgment as the terrible analogue of preservation; (3) every sacrifice was required to be salted (Leviticus 2:13: "every offering of grain shall be seasoned with salt") — the disciples' lives as sacrificial offerings salted with the fire of suffering for Christ. The connection between fire (Mark 9:43–48) and salt (Mark 9:49–50) is the verbal bridge; the full semantic connection remains debated across the tradition.
50: Kalon to halas — ean de to halas analon genētai, en tini auto artyesete? — salt is good — but if the salt becomes saltless, with what will you season it? — halas (salt) carries multiple registers: preservation (against decay), covenant (berit melach, the covenant of salt — Leviticus 2:13; Numbers 18:19), and quality/flavor. analon (without salt, saltless — the alpha-privative of halas) describes salt that has lost its defining property: the rhetorical question has no answer, because saltless salt cannot be re-salted by any process. echete en heautois hala kai eirēneuete en allēlois — have salt in yourselves and be at peace with one another — the closing application: internal saltness (the quality of covenant-loyalty and preserving fidelity) is the precondition for community peace. The chapter that opened with Transfiguration-glory on the mountain ends with the homely image of salt and the command for peace among the disciples who had been arguing about greatness on the road.
